items.q: Why do people move from rural to urban areas in LICs?
items.correct: A combination of push factors (rural poverty, lack of services, agricultural change) and pull factors (perceived jobs, education, healthcare) — though reality often disappoints.
items.misconception: Because cities in poor countries are always better than the countryside.
items.random_wrong: Because the government forces them to move to cities.
items.why: Migrants often end up in shanty towns worse than what they left. The decision is based on perception of opportunity, not guaranteed improvement — a key nuance students miss.

items.q: Why do earthquakes typically cause more deaths in LICs than HICs?
items.correct: LICs often have poorly enforced building codes, weak emergency response, dense informal housing and limited preparedness — not because the earthquakes are stronger.
items.misconception: LICs have more earthquake deaths because more earthquakes happen there.
items.random_wrong: HICs never have earthquake deaths because of advanced technology.
items.why: Vulnerability is socio-economic, not seismic. Japan and Haiti both sit on plate boundaries; the death toll difference comes from building quality, planning and response — a key development comparison.

items.q: What is the difference between the Burgess and Hoyt urban land use models?
items.correct: Burgess (1925) describes concentric rings radiating from the CBD; Hoyt (1939) describes sectors (wedges) extending along transport routes — recognising how transport shapes urban growth.
items.misconception: Burgess and Hoyt models show the same thing in different drawings.
items.random_wrong: Burgess is for American cities and Hoyt is for European cities.
items.why: The models differ structurally: rings vs wedges. Both were derived from Chicago. Real cities have elements of both — modern models like Mann combine them with sector wedges within concentric zones.

items.q: Which African region suffers severe desertification?
items.why: It's Sahel. “Maghreb” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the maximum rainfall in hot deserts?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 250mm. “100mm” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What percentage of Northern Hemisphere is permafrost?
items.why: Exact answer: 25 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
